---
title: "Spectral geometry: manifold harmonics and embedding"
---

::: {.callout-tldr title="Fast path"}
The eigenfunctions of the Laplace–Beltrami operator $\Delta = M^{-1}L$ are the
natural **vibration modes** of a surface — *Fourier on a manifold*. They form an
orthonormal basis ordered by smoothness: $\phi_0$ is the constant
($\lambda_0 = 0$), then increasingly wiggly harmonics. Two payoffs follow.

- The low modes are the smoothest non-constant functions on the surface, so any
  filter that reweights them by a function of frequency is a mesh operation — and
  the geodesic smoothing of the [previous page](laplace-beltrami.qmd#sec-smoothing)
  *is* a low-pass filter in this basis.
- On the round sphere the first three non-constant eigenfunctions **are** the
  coordinate functions $x, y, z$. So embedding each vertex by $(\phi_1,\phi_2,\phi_3)$
  and normalising maps a genus-0 surface onto the sphere **in one eigensolve** —
  the recon-surf "spectral inflation" implemented by `spectral_sphere_embedding`.
:::

This page sits on the spectral rung of the unit, immediately after
[the Laplace–Beltrami operator](laplace-beltrami.qmd#sec-cotangent). It assumes you
are comfortable with **eigendecomposition** — $A x = \lambda x$, orthogonal
eigenvectors of a symmetric matrix, the generalised problem $A x = \lambda B x$ —
but have had **no formal training in differential geometry or numerical
eigensolvers**. We build everything from the discrete Fourier basis you already
know, then show how `nitrix` resolves it on real meshes without ever forming a
dense matrix.

The unit's governing idea runs straight through here. A triangle mesh is a
**discrete approximation of a smooth surface**; its eigenpairs
$(\lambda_k, \phi_k)$ approximate the eigenpairs of the continuous Laplace–Beltrami
operator, and they **converge to that continuous truth under refinement**. We will
not check this with bit-parity against any reference implementation — the right
posture is the same `metrics↔ITK` one used throughout the unit: validate against
an **analytic oracle** (the sphere, whose spectrum and harmonics are known in
closed form) by **class and correlation**, not equality.

## A motivating question {#sec-motivation}

You have reconstructed a **cortical surface** — a genus-0 triangle mesh wrapping
one hemisphere, the input to every surface-based analysis. Two questions recur, and
both are spectral.

First, **how do I compare shapes?** Two cortices, or one cortex before and after
preprocessing, are different tessellations with no vertex correspondence. You want a
fingerprint that depends on the *shape* and not on the meshing — ideally one that is
invariant to rotation, translation, and even to remeshing. The eigenvalues
$\lambda_0 \le \lambda_1 \le \dots$ of $\Delta$ are exactly such a fingerprint (the
**Shape-DNA**), because they are determined by the surface's intrinsic geometry, not
by how you happened to triangulate it.

Second, **how do I put two cortices into correspondence at all?** The workhorse
answer is to map each hemisphere onto a common sphere and register there. The first
step of that pipeline — turning an inflated cortical surface into a near-spherical
map — is, remarkably, a single eigensolve. The first three non-constant harmonics
of the cortex behave like the sphere's own $x, y, z$, so they *are* an embedding
onto the sphere. We return to this in @sec-embedding; it is the flagship routine of
this page.

Both questions are answered by the eigenstructure of the operator the
[last page](laplace-beltrami.qmd#sec-cotangent) built. We now open it up.

## Eigenfunctions as manifold harmonics {#sec-eigen}

### Start from the basis you already know

Take the unit interval $[0, 1]$ and the humble second-derivative operator
$-\partial_x^2$, with the function pinned to zero at both ends (a guitar string
clamped at the bridge and the nut). Ask the eigenvalue question: which functions are
sent back to a scalar multiple of themselves?

$$
-\partial_x^2 \, u = \lambda \, u, \qquad u(0) = u(1) = 0.
$$ {#eq-interval-eig}

You can write the answer down from memory. The functions that survive double
differentiation up to a sign are sines and cosines, and the boundary condition keeps
only the sines that vanish at both ends:

$$
u_k(x) = \sin(k\pi x), \qquad \lambda_k = (k\pi)^2, \qquad k = 1, 2, 3, \dots
$$ {#eq-interval-modes}

This is the **Fourier sine basis**, and the eigenvalue $\lambda_k = (k\pi)^2$ is a
direct measure of **frequency**: small $k$ is a slow, smooth half-wave; large $k$
oscillates fast. Two facts will carry over and are worth naming now. The
eigenfunctions are **orthogonal** ($\int_0^1 \sin(j\pi x)\sin(k\pi x)\,dx = 0$ for
$j \ne k$), and they are **ordered by smoothness**: $\lambda_k$ is, up to a
constant, the average squared slope $\int (u_k')^2 / \int u_k^2$, so "small
eigenvalue" literally means "few wiggles". The whole of Fourier analysis is: expand
a signal in this basis, and you have sorted it by frequency.

### The same question on a curved surface

Now replace the interval with a surface and $-\partial_x^2$ with the
Laplace–Beltrami operator $\Delta$ — the [previous page's](laplace-beltrami.qmd#sec-cotangent)
"how much does a field differ from its local average, measured along the surface".
The eigenvalue question is identical in spirit: which fields on the surface are sent
back to a multiple of themselves by $\Delta$?

There is one wrinkle, and it is the entire reason the discrete problem is a
*generalised* eigenproblem. On a mesh, $\Delta$ does not arrive as a single matrix.
It factors as $\Delta = M^{-1}L$: the symmetric positive-semidefinite **cotangent
stiffness** $L$ (the metric / shape part) divided by the diagonal **lumped mass**
$M = \operatorname{diag}(A_i)$ (the per-vertex area). Substituting and clearing the
inverse mass:

$$
\Delta \phi_k = \lambda_k \phi_k
\;\Longleftrightarrow\;
M^{-1} L \phi_k = \lambda_k \phi_k
\;\Longleftrightarrow\;
\boxed{\,L \phi_k = \lambda_k M \phi_k\,}.
$$ {#eq-gen-eig}

The boxed form is the **generalised symmetric eigenproblem** $L\phi = \lambda M\phi$,
and it is the discrete realisation of the continuous Laplace–Beltrami eigenproblem.
Reading the symbols: $L$ is the stiffness assembled by
[`mesh_cotangent_laplacian`](laplace-beltrami.qmd#sec-cotangent), $M$ is the mass
from [`vertex_areas`](laplace-beltrami.qmd#sec-mass), $\phi_k \in \mathbb{R}^n$ is
the $k$-th eigenfunction sampled at the vertices, and $\lambda_k \ge 0$ is its
frequency. The mass appears on the right because orthogonality on a curved surface
is **area-weighted**: the natural inner product is
$\langle \phi_j, \phi_k\rangle_M = \phi_j^\top M \phi_k$, and the eigenfunctions are
orthonormal in *that* product, not the plain Euclidean one. (This is the discrete
version of "integrate over the surface", $\int_{\mathcal{S}} \phi_j \phi_k \, dA$.)

These $\phi_k$ are the **manifold harmonics**: a shape-intrinsic Fourier basis. As
on the interval, $\lambda_0 = 0$ with $\phi_0$ the constant (the Laplacian sends
constants to zero — every row of $L$ sums to zero, which the
[previous page](laplace-beltrami.qmd#sec-cotangent) made the diagonal carry), then
$\lambda_1 \le \lambda_2 \le \dots$ in order of increasing wiggliness. @fig-spectral-eigenfunctions
shows the first four on a subdivided icosphere: the flat constant, then the three
harmonics that are visibly the $x$, $y$, and $z$ coordinate fields.

```{python}
#| label: fig-spectral-eigenfunctions
#| fig-cap: "The first four manifold harmonics of an icosphere, computed by the
#|   generalised LBO eigenproblem $L\\phi=\\lambda M\\phi$ on a hand-built mesh.
#|   $\\phi_0$ is the constant ($\\lambda_0=0$); $\\phi_1,\\phi_2,\\phi_3$ are
#|   the degree-1 spherical harmonics — visibly the coordinate functions
#|   $x,y,z$. The colour is the eigenfunction value."
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from mpl_toolkits.mplot3d.art3d import Poly3DCollection

# ---- Build an icosphere by hand (icosahedron + edge-midpoint subdivision) ----
def icosahedron():
    t = (1.0 + np.sqrt(5.0)) / 2.0
    v = np.array([
        [-1, t, 0], [1, t, 0], [-1, -t, 0], [1, -t, 0],
        [0, -1, t], [0, 1, t], [0, -1, -t], [0, 1, -t],
        [t, 0, -1], [t, 0, 1], [-t, 0, -1], [-t, 0, 1],
    ], dtype=float)
    f = np.array([
        [0, 11, 5], [0, 5, 1], [0, 1, 7], [0, 7, 10], [0, 10, 11],
        [1, 5, 9], [5, 11, 4], [11, 10, 2], [10, 7, 6], [7, 1, 8],
        [3, 9, 4], [3, 4, 2], [3, 2, 6], [3, 6, 8], [3, 8, 9],
        [4, 9, 5], [2, 4, 11], [6, 2, 10], [8, 6, 7], [9, 8, 1],
    ], dtype=int)
    v /= np.linalg.norm(v, axis=1, keepdims=True)
    return v, f

def subdivide(v, f):
    mid = {}
    v = list(v)
    def midpoint(a, b):
        key = (min(a, b), max(a, b))
        if key in mid:
            return mid[key]
        m = (np.asarray(v[a]) + np.asarray(v[b])) / 2.0
        m /= np.linalg.norm(m)
        v.append(m)
        mid[key] = len(v) - 1
        return mid[key]
    nf = []
    for a, b, c in f:
        ab = midpoint(a, b); bc = midpoint(b, c); ca = midpoint(c, a)
        nf += [[a, ab, ca], [b, bc, ab], [c, ca, bc], [ab, bc, ca]]
    return np.array(v), np.array(nf)

def icosphere(level):
    v, f = icosahedron()
    for _ in range(level):
        v, f = subdivide(v, f)
    return v, f

V, F = icosphere(3)
n = V.shape[0]

# ---- Cotangent stiffness L (PSD) + barycentric lumped mass diagonal A ----
def cotangent_system(V, F):
    L = np.zeros((n, n))
    A = np.zeros(n)
    for i, j, k in F:
        cross = np.cross(V[j] - V[i], V[k] - V[i])
        area = 0.5 * np.linalg.norm(cross)
        A[i] += area / 3.0; A[j] += area / 3.0; A[k] += area / 3.0
        # cot of the angle at p, opposite edge (q, r), added to that edge
        for (p, q, r) in [(i, j, k), (j, k, i), (k, i, j)]:
            u = V[q] - V[p]; w = V[r] - V[p]
            cot = np.dot(u, w) / (np.linalg.norm(np.cross(u, w)) + 1e-30)
            wt = 0.5 * cot
            L[q, r] -= wt; L[r, q] -= wt
            L[q, q] += wt; L[r, r] += wt
    return L, A

L, A = cotangent_system(V, F)

# ---- Generalised eigenproblem via symmetric whitening L~ = M^-1/2 L M^-1/2 ----
inv_sqrt = 1.0 / np.sqrt(A)
Lt = (inv_sqrt[:, None] * L) * inv_sqrt[None, :]
Lt = 0.5 * (Lt + Lt.T)
w, U = np.linalg.eigh(Lt)
order = np.argsort(w)
phi = inv_sqrt[:, None] * U[:, order]   # generalised eigenfunctions

tris = V[F]
fig = plt.figure(figsize=(11, 3))
titles = [r"$\phi_0$ (constant, $\lambda_0=0$)",
          r"$\phi_1 \sim x$", r"$\phi_2 \sim y$", r"$\phi_3 \sim z$"]
for col in range(4):
    ax = fig.add_subplot(1, 4, col + 1, projection="3d")
    face_c = phi[:, col][F].mean(axis=1)
    norm = plt.Normalize(face_c.min(), face_c.max())
    colors = plt.cm.coolwarm(norm(face_c))
    ax.add_collection3d(Poly3DCollection(tris, facecolors=colors, edgecolors="none"))
    ax.set_xlim(-1, 1); ax.set_ylim(-1, 1); ax.set_zlim(-1, 1)
    ax.set_box_aspect((1, 1, 1)); ax.set_axis_off()
    ax.set_title(titles[col], fontsize=9)
    ax.view_init(elev=20, azim=35)
fig.suptitle("Low-frequency manifold harmonics of the icosphere", fontsize=11)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

### The spectrum is an isometry invariant (Shape-DNA)

Here is why the eigenvalues answer the first motivating question. The generalised
problem @eq-gen-eig depends on the mesh **only through $L$ and $M$**, and both are
built from intrinsic quantities — edge cotangents and triangle areas — that do not
change if you rotate, translate, or reflect the surface in space. They do not even
depend on how the surface is *embedded* in $\mathbb{R}^3$, only on its internal
metric. So the sequence $\lambda_0, \lambda_1, \lambda_2, \dots$ is an **isometry
invariant**: bend a sheet of paper without stretching it and the spectrum is
unchanged. Reuter, Wolter and Peinecke (2006) named the truncated spectrum the
**Shape-DNA** and used it precisely as a shape fingerprint.

Two honest caveats keep this practitioner-grade. The spectrum is invariant under
isometry but it is *not* a complete invariant — rare "isospectral" shapes share a
spectrum without being congruent ("can you hear the shape of a drum?" — no, not
always). And on a *discrete* mesh the invariance is only approximate: it holds in the
limit of refinement, and a coarse or anisotropic tessellation perturbs the high
eigenvalues first. This is the convergence-under-refinement theme again — the low
spectrum is robust and trustworthy; trust the tail less.

### The federation link: this is HSGP's closed-surface twin {#sec-hsgp-link}

If you have read the stats unit, @eq-gen-eig should feel familiar, because you have
met it before in a different costume. The **Hilbert-space approximate Gaussian
process** ([HSGP](../hsgp.qmd#sec-hsgp)) approximates a GP by expanding it in the
**eigenfunctions of the Laplacian on a bounded interval** — which, by
@eq-interval-modes, are exactly $\sin(k\pi x)$ — and then reweighting mode $k$ by the
**spectral density** of the kernel evaluated at the eigenvalue, $S(\sqrt{\lambda_k})$.

That is the same construction as here, term for term:

- a **Laplacian eigenbasis** $\{\phi_k\}$ ordered by frequency $\lambda_k$ —
  $\sin(k\pi x)$ on the interval, the manifold harmonics on the surface;
- a **spectral reweighting** $g(\lambda_k)$ that picks out which frequencies to keep
  — the GP spectral density in HSGP, a low-pass damping in surface smoothing
  (@sec-heat-kernel).

The only difference is the **domain**: HSGP lives on a box with chosen boundary
conditions, manifold harmonics live on a closed curved surface where the geometry
*supplies* the operator. Same idea, different manifold. Reading the two pages
together is the fastest way to internalise "eigenfunctions of a Laplacian as a basis,
reweighted by a spectral density" as one technique with two homes.

## The heat kernel and spectral filtering {#sec-heat-kernel}

### Diffusion is diagonal in the eigenbasis

The [previous page](laplace-beltrami.qmd#sec-smoothing) smoothed a field by letting
heat diffuse along the surface. Let us see what that does *to each harmonic*, because
the answer makes the whole spectral picture click.

Heat flow is the equation $\partial_t u = -\Delta u$: every point relaxes toward its
local average, and the formal solution after time $t$ is $u(t) = e^{-t\Delta} u(0)$.
The exponential of an operator is intimidating in general, but in the eigenbasis it
collapses to scalar arithmetic, because $\Delta$ acts on $\phi_k$ as plain
multiplication by $\lambda_k$. Expand the initial field in harmonics,
$u(0) = \sum_k c_k \phi_k$. Then each coefficient decays on its own:

$$
e^{-t\Delta}\,\phi_k = e^{-t\lambda_k}\,\phi_k
\;\;\Longrightarrow\;\;
u(t) = \sum_k c_k \, e^{-t\lambda_k}\,\phi_k.
$$ {#eq-heat-kernel}

Read @eq-heat-kernel as a recipe: diffusion **multiplies mode $k$ by
$e^{-t\lambda_k}$**. The constant ($\lambda_0 = 0$) is untouched — heat conserves
the mean. Low frequencies (small $\lambda_k$) are barely damped; high frequencies
(large $\lambda_k$) are crushed exponentially. That is the precise sense in which
**geodesic smoothing is a low-pass filter on manifold harmonics**: the damping
profile $g(\lambda) = e^{-t\lambda}$ is the transfer function, and $t$ sets the
cutoff. This is the closed-surface analogue of the HSGP spectral density from
@sec-hsgp-link — there the kernel chooses $g$; here the diffusion time does.

### What `surface_smooth` actually computes, in spectral terms

`nitrix` does **not** form the eigenbasis to smooth. Computing all of $e^{-t\Delta}$
through @eq-heat-kernel would need every eigenpair — wasteful, and the high modes are
exactly the ones a discrete mesh resolves worst. Instead
[`surface_smooth`](laplace-beltrami.qmd#sec-smoothing) takes **one backward-Euler
step**: it solves the sparse SPD system

$$
(M + t L)\,x = M\,x_0,
\qquad t = \frac{\mathrm{FWHM}^2}{16\ln 2},
$$ {#eq-backward-euler}

by matrix-free conjugate gradients, never materialising a dense operator. The link to
the spectral view is exact and worth making explicit. Plug a single harmonic
$x_0 = \phi_k$ into @eq-backward-euler. Using $L\phi_k = \lambda_k M\phi_k$, the mass
cancels and the harmonic passes through with gain

$$
\hat{g}(\lambda_k) = \frac{1}{1 + t\lambda_k}.
$$ {#eq-bweuler-transfer}

So backward-Euler is *also* a low-pass filter on the manifold harmonics — its
transfer function $\frac{1}{1+t\lambda}$ is the rational (Padé) approximation of the
true heat damping $e^{-t\lambda}$ from @eq-heat-kernel. They agree to first order in
$t\lambda$, both fix the constant, both monotonically suppress high modes; the
backward-Euler version is unconditionally stable and costs one CG solve instead of an
eigendecomposition. This is why the previous page could implement diffusion without
ever mentioning eigenfunctions: the eigenbasis is the *explanation*, the sparse solve
is the *implementation*.

The general principle behind all of this: **any spectral filter $g(\lambda_k)$ is a
mesh operation**. Reweight each harmonic by $g$ and you have a band-pass, a
sharpening filter, a Tikhonov regulariser, or a GP prior — all without leaving the
mesh. `nitrix` ships the two filters its pipelines need (the heat low-pass via
`surface_smooth`, and the implicit embedding filter of @sec-embedding) rather than a
general spectral-filter entry point; if you need an arbitrary $g$, assemble it from
the eigenpairs that @sec-embedding's solver returns.

## Spectral embedding: a sphere in one eigensolve {#sec-embedding}

This is the flagship. We now answer the second motivating question — turning a
genus-0 cortical surface into a spherical map — and we do it with a single call to
[`spectral_sphere_embedding`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/geometry/sphere.py),
the FastSurfer / recon-surf "spectral inflation" verified against `Deep-MI/FastSurfer
recon_surf/spherically_project.py`.

### Why the first three harmonics *are* the sphere

Run @fig-spectral-eigenfunctions back through your eye. On the round sphere, $\phi_1$,
$\phi_2$, $\phi_3$ were not merely *some* smooth functions — they were the coordinate
fields $x$, $y$, $z$ themselves. This is not a coincidence of the icosphere; it is a
theorem. The degree-one spherical harmonics are exactly the restrictions of $x$, $y$,
$z$ to the sphere, they share the same Laplace eigenvalue, and they span the first
non-trivial eigenspace. So on the sphere, **the map "vertex $\mapsto$ its first three
harmonic values" is the identity** (up to scale and a choice of axes).

The leap — the discrete realisation of the **uniformisation theorem**, which says any
genus-0 surface is conformally a sphere — is that for a surface that is merely
*close* to a sphere (an inflated cortex), its first three harmonics are *close* to
the sphere's $x, y, z$. So the embedding

$$
v_i \;\longmapsto\; r \cdot \frac{(\phi_1, \phi_2, \phi_3)_i}{\lVert(\phi_1,\phi_2,\phi_3)_i\rVert}
$$ {#eq-embedding}

— take each vertex's first three non-constant harmonic values as a 3-vector and
normalise it to radius $r$ — lands every vertex on the sphere, in **one eigensolve,
no iteration**. The constant $\phi_0$ is dropped (it carries no positional
information), and the normalisation projects radially onto the sphere. That is the
entirety of `spectral_sphere_embedding`'s mathematical content.

### The numerical problem: the smallest eigenvalues are clustered

You might now reach for a dense `eigh`, take the four smallest eigenpairs, and be
done. On a real cortex with hundreds of thousands of vertices that is a non-starter —
a dense $n \times n$ factorisation is out of memory and out of time — so we need an
**iterative** solver that touches the operator only through matrix–vector products.
And iterative solvers have a specific weakness that bites *exactly here*: they
converge fast on **well-separated extremal** eigenvalues and slowly on **clustered**
ones. The eigenvalues we want — $\lambda_0, \dots, \lambda_3$ — are the *smallest*,
and on a near-sphere they are tightly bunched just above zero. That is the worst case.

`spectral_sphere_embedding` defeats this with three moves, each fixing the previous
one's flaw. We walk the real mechanics from the source.

**Move 1 — symmetrise the generalised problem.** Iterative eigensolvers want a single
symmetric operator, but @eq-gen-eig has the mass on the right. Because $M$ is diagonal
and positive, we can absorb it by a change of variables $\psi = M^{1/2}\phi$, which
turns $L\phi = \lambda M\phi$ into the ordinary symmetric problem
$\tilde L \psi = \lambda \psi$ with

$$
\tilde L = M^{-1/2} L M^{-1/2}.
$$ {#eq-symmetrise}

In code this is one line: `lt_vals = lap.values * inv_sqrt[:, None] *
inv_sqrt[lap.indices]`, scaling each stored ELL entry by the inverse-square-root mass
of its row vertex and its column vertex. $\tilde L$ shares its eigenvalues with
$\Delta$, and recovering the eigenfunctions afterwards is the single un-whitening
$\phi = M^{-1/2}\psi$ (`phi = inv_sqrt[:, None] * psi`).

**Move 2 — flip smallest into largest with an affinity.** Iterative methods find the
*largest* eigenpairs easily; we want the *smallest*. The fix is to build an
**affinity** whose large eigenpairs are $\tilde L$'s small ones:

$$
A = I - \tilde L / c,
$$ {#eq-affinity}

where $c$ is a **Gershgorin bound** on $\tilde L$'s largest eigenvalue (`c =
jnp.max(jnp.sum(jnp.abs(lt_vals), axis=1))` — the largest absolute row sum, which by
Gershgorin's circle theorem dominates the spectrum). Dividing by $c$ scales the
spectrum into $[0, 1]$, and subtracting from the identity *reverses the order*:
$\tilde L$'s smallest eigenvalue (near $0$) becomes $A$'s largest (near $1$). The
construction adds the identity onto the diagonal, which the cotangent ELL stores at
**column 0** (the diagonal-first layout the
[previous page](laplace-beltrami.qmd#sec-cotangent) established) — the source
*asserts* this layout rather than assuming it (`assert bool(jnp.all(lap.indices[:, 0]
== jnp.arange(n)))`) before doing `aff_vals = (-lt_vals / c).at[:, 0].add(1.0)`.

**Move 3 — shift-invert to pry apart the cluster.** Reversing the order is not
enough, because $A$'s largest eigenvalues are still *clustered near 1* (they are
$1$ minus the clustered small $\lambda$'s, so the bunching is merely shifted, not
cured). A plain power-style reflection therefore
cannot separate $\phi_0$ from $\phi_1, \phi_2, \phi_3$ in a reasonable number of
iterations, and the recovered "first three" come out contaminated — and a
contaminated embedding **folds**: triangles turn inside out. This is the load-bearing
measured finding behind the design: a plain spectral reflection folds **~47% of
triangles** on real surfaces, because the gaps it needs are vanishingly small.

The cure is **shift-invert**. Instead of iterating on $A$, iterate on
$(\tilde L - \sigma I)^{-1}$ for a small negative shift $\sigma$ (here
$\sigma = -0.01$). Inversion maps the eigenvalues nearest $\sigma$ to the *largest*
magnitudes, and — crucially — it **amplifies their relative gaps**: two eigenvalues
that differ by a hair near zero differ by a chasm after inversion. The clustered
modes that defeated the plain method are now the well-separated extremal ones an
iterative solver loves. The inverse is never formed; it is applied through an inner
**conjugate-gradient** solve, so the whole thing stays matrix-free. @fig-spectral-embedding
shows the difference directly: the plain-reflection embedding folds a visible fraction
of its triangles, while the shift-invert embedding is essentially fold-free.

`nitrix` expresses moves 2–3 through the eigensolver of the
[solvers page](solvers.qmd#sec-eigensolve),
[`eigsolve_top_k`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/linalg/_eigsolve.py),
driven by a frozen `SolverSpec`:

```python
pair = eigsolve_top_k(
    affinity,                                   # A = I - L~/c, an ELL operator
    n_eig,                                      # over-sample beyond the 4 needed
    spec=SolverSpec.shift_invert(
        sigma=-0.01, outer_iters=eig_iters, cg_iters=eig_iters,
    ),
)
lam   = c * (1.0 - pair.values)                 # recover L~ eigenvalues
order = jnp.argsort(lam)                         # ascending by frequency
psi   = pair.vectors[:, order]
phi   = inv_sqrt[:, None] * psi                  # back to LBO eigenfunctions
emb   = phi[:, 1:4]                              # drop phi_0, keep phi_1..phi_3
```

Note `n_eig` (default 10, must be $\ge 4$) **over-samples**: requesting more pairs
than the four you need gives LOBPCG extra subspace room and speeds convergence on the
clustered low spectrum. After the solve, the affinity eigenvalues are converted back
to $\tilde L$ eigenvalues by inverting @eq-affinity (`lam = c * (1.0 - pair.values)`),
sorted ascending, the eigenfunctions un-whitened, the constant dropped, and the
remaining three normalised per @eq-embedding.

### The solver underneath: `eigsolve_top_k` and `SolverSpec`

A short tour of the machinery, since this page is the spectral surface's primary
consumer of it (full treatment on the [solvers page](solvers.qmd#sec-eigensolve)).

[`SolverSpec`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/linalg/_eigsolve.py)
is a **frozen, hashable** dataclass carrying all static solver configuration. Its
hashability is load-bearing: the whole spec rides `jax.custom_vjp`'s
`nondiff_argnums` as a single argument, so the iterative forward branches on
`spec.method` at trace time. Build it through its classmethods, never the raw
constructor:

- `SolverSpec.auto()` — the default; resolves to dense `eigh` for a materialised
  matrix, `lobpcg` for a sparse operand. (Tag: this is the policy
  `spectral_sphere_embedding` overrides.)
- `SolverSpec.eigh()` — dense full spectrum sliced to the top-$k$; **dense-only** (it
  materialises the whole spectrum), unusable on a full cortex.
- `SolverSpec.lobpcg(n_iters, tol, …)` — plain LOBPCG; the benchmarked sparse default
  for *extremal* spectra that are already well-separated.
- `SolverSpec.shift_invert(sigma, outer_iters, cg_iters, …)` — the one this page
  uses; LOBPCG on $(\,\tilde L - \sigma I)^{-1}$ via inner CG, for the *clustered*
  small spectrum.
- `SolverSpec.poly(degree, shift, …)` — a shifted-power polynomial spectral filter,
  the alternative cluster-amplifier when an inner CG solve is undesirable.

A subtle but important flag is `promise_symmetry` (default `True`). When the stored
operator is genuinely symmetric — as the cotangent affinity on a regular mesh is —
the forward applies its matvec directly. When it is not (e.g. an affinity sparsified
by top-$k$-per-row, whose pattern is asymmetric), set `promise_symmetry=False` and the
ELL / SectionedELL forward applies the symmetric part $\tfrac12(AX + A^\top X)$
instead. The embedding leaves it at the default because the cotangent affinity is
symmetric by construction.

[`eigsolve_top_k`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/linalg/_eigsolve.py)
itself returns an **`EigPair(values, vectors)`** named tuple, eigenvalues
*largest-first*, and accepts dense, `ELL`, or `SectionedELL` operands — the same
storage formats the [operators page](operators.qmd#sec-ell) defined. It is
**differentiable w.r.t. the operator** for every method, via a single shared implicit
VJP: the gradient depends only on the converged eigenpair, not on which solver
produced it, so one backward serves all methods, specialised only by storage format
(dense $VKV^\top$; ELL projection onto the sparsity pattern; per-section for
SectionedELL). This is the same differentiable-eigensolver that the
[graph-geometry page](graph-geometry.qmd#sec-spectral-graph) uses for learned
connectopies — there you *do* backpropagate through the eigensolve; here you do not.

### Execution classes — and why this one is not differentiable

The pieces of the embedding fall into the unit's three execution classes, and the
distinction is not academic — it dictates what you may wrap in `jax.jit`.

- **DIFF-JAX** (jittable + differentiable). `signed_spherical_areas` (below) and the
  affinity matvec are pure JAX. The eigensolve `eigsolve_top_k` is differentiable
  w.r.t. its operator.
- **HOST-CTOR** (data-dependent host construction). The cotangent operator inside
  `spectral_sphere_embedding` is assembled host-side by `mesh_cotangent_laplacian`
  (NumPy), exactly as on the [previous page](laplace-beltrami.qmd#sec-cotangent). You
  can pass `laplacian=` to **reuse** an already-assembled operator — which
  `spherical_parameterize` does, so the costly host assembly happens once per call,
  not twice.
- **HOST-QA** (host query / decision). `is_bijective_sphere_map` (below) returns a
  Python `bool`.

The consequence, stated plainly in the source: `spectral_sphere_embedding` is **not
differentiable w.r.t. `mesh.vertices`**, because the cotangent weights are built
host-side as constants. It is an **init / preprocessing artefact** — call it to
*produce* a spherical map; do not wrap it in `jax.grad`. This is the unit-wide rule
**never call a HOST op inside a jitted loop**: the host construction must happen once,
outside any traced region, and the device-resident eigensolve and area checks run on
the result. (A footnote in the source warns of a further wrinkle: the shift-invert
core is matrix-free, but its orthonormalisation shares the dense solver-handle pool,
so on a box whose pool is dead the eigensolve falls back to CPU — correct, though not
GPU-resident there.)

### Validate, do not assume: bijectivity by signed area

The embedding is explicitly **not guaranteed bijective** — it is the recon-surf-grade
one-shot map, and a tiny flipped fraction may remain even with shift-invert. So
bijectivity is **checked, not assumed**, and the check is a clean instance of the
unit's analytic-oracle posture. The oracle is **Gauss–Bonnet for the sphere**: a
genuine degree-1 cover of the unit sphere subtends a total solid angle of exactly
$4\pi$ steradians.

[`signed_spherical_areas`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/geometry/sphere.py)
computes, for each triangle, the **signed solid angle** it subtends at the origin —
the area of the spherical triangle on the unit sphere, with a sign:

$$
\Omega_f = 2\,\operatorname{atan2}\!\big(\,a\cdot(b\times c),\;
\lvert a\rvert\lvert b\rvert\lvert c\rvert + (a\cdot b)\lvert c\rvert
+ (b\cdot c)\lvert a\rvert + (c\cdot a)\lvert b\rvert \,\big),
$$ {#eq-solid-angle}

the **Van Oosterom–Strackee** formula, for triangle vertices $a, b, c$. It is
radius-independent (a solid angle does not care about sphere size), pure JAX, and
differentiable. The load-bearing part is the **sign**: it is the orientation of the
triangle (the triple product $a\cdot(b\times c)$). A correctly oriented triangle has
positive signed area; a **folded** triangle — one turned inside out by a bad
embedding — has **negative** signed area. That single sign is how a fold announces
itself, and it is what @fig-spectral-embedding colours by.

[`is_bijective_sphere_map`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/geometry/sphere.py)
turns these into a host decision (**HOST-QA**, a Python `bool`). The map passes iff
two conditions hold:

- **degree-1 cover**: the signed areas sum to within `total_tol` of $\pm 4\pi$ — the
  Gauss–Bonnet oracle; and
- **fold-free**: the fraction of solid angle in triangles flipped against the
  dominant orientation is $\le$ `flip_area_tol`. With `flip_area_tol=0` this is the
  strict no-fold test; a small tolerance (the source notes `8e-4`) mirrors the
  FastSurfer / recon-surf quality gate, which tolerates a vanishing flipped fraction.

The intended workflow is therefore: run `spectral_sphere_embedding`, gate with
`is_bijective_sphere_map`, and if it fails fall back to a guaranteed-bijective init or
refine. One gauge is deliberately *not* resolved here: the eigenfunction
**sign and ordering** are arbitrary (nitrix is orientation-agnostic; an eigenvector
can flip sign or two equal-eigenvalue modes can swap), so the anatomical sign/axis
alignment is a downstream consumer step.

```{python}
#| label: fig-spectral-embedding
#| fig-cap: "Spectral spherical embedding of a gently deformed genus-0 surface.
#|   Left: a plain affinity reflection (fixed-iteration power method) cannot
#|   separate the clustered smallest eigenvalues, so the first three harmonics
#|   are contaminated and many triangles fold (red = negative signed area).
#|   Middle: shift-invert amplifies the eigenvalue gaps and the same iteration
#|   budget yields an essentially fold-free map. Right: the distribution of
#|   per-triangle signed solid angles — the plain map has a negative tail, the
#|   shift-invert map does not. Pure-numpy reproduction of the mechanism in
#|   `spectral_sphere_embedding`."
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from mpl_toolkits.mplot3d.art3d import Poly3DCollection

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)

def icosahedron():
    t = (1.0 + np.sqrt(5.0)) / 2.0
    v = np.array([
        [-1, t, 0], [1, t, 0], [-1, -t, 0], [1, -t, 0],
        [0, -1, t], [0, 1, t], [0, -1, -t], [0, 1, -t],
        [t, 0, -1], [t, 0, 1], [-t, 0, -1], [-t, 0, 1],
    ], dtype=float)
    f = np.array([
        [0, 11, 5], [0, 5, 1], [0, 1, 7], [0, 7, 10], [0, 10, 11],
        [1, 5, 9], [5, 11, 4], [11, 10, 2], [10, 7, 6], [7, 1, 8],
        [3, 9, 4], [3, 4, 2], [3, 2, 6], [3, 6, 8], [3, 8, 9],
        [4, 9, 5], [2, 4, 11], [6, 2, 10], [8, 6, 7], [9, 8, 1],
    ], dtype=int)
    v /= np.linalg.norm(v, axis=1, keepdims=True)
    return v, f

def subdivide(v, f):
    mid = {}; v = list(v)
    def midpoint(a, b):
        key = (min(a, b), max(a, b))
        if key in mid: return mid[key]
        m = (np.asarray(v[a]) + np.asarray(v[b])) / 2.0
        m /= np.linalg.norm(m); v.append(m); mid[key] = len(v) - 1
        return mid[key]
    nf = []
    for a, b, c in f:
        ab = midpoint(a, b); bc = midpoint(b, c); ca = midpoint(c, a)
        nf += [[a, ab, ca], [b, bc, ab], [c, ca, bc], [ab, bc, ca]]
    return np.array(v), np.array(nf)

def icosphere(level):
    v, f = icosahedron()
    for _ in range(level): v, f = subdivide(v, f)
    return v, f

# A mildly deformed near-spherical genus-0 surface (so the map is non-trivial).
V0, F = icosphere(2)
defo = 1.0 + 0.18 * V0[:, 0] * V0[:, 1] + 0.12 * V0[:, 2] ** 2
V = V0 * defo[:, None]
n = V.shape[0]

def cotangent_system(V, F):
    L = np.zeros((n, n)); A = np.zeros(n)
    for i, j, k in F:
        area = 0.5 * np.linalg.norm(np.cross(V[j] - V[i], V[k] - V[i]))
        A[i] += area / 3; A[j] += area / 3; A[k] += area / 3
        for (p, q, r) in [(i, j, k), (j, k, i), (k, i, j)]:
            u = V[q] - V[p]; w = V[r] - V[p]
            cot = np.dot(u, w) / (np.linalg.norm(np.cross(u, w)) + 1e-30)
            wt = 0.5 * cot
            L[q, r] -= wt; L[r, q] -= wt; L[q, q] += wt; L[r, r] += wt
    return L, A

L, A = cotangent_system(V, F)
inv_sqrt = 1.0 / np.sqrt(A)
Lt = (inv_sqrt[:, None] * L) * inv_sqrt[None, :]
Lt = 0.5 * (Lt + Lt.T)
c = np.max(np.sum(np.abs(Lt), axis=1))            # Gershgorin lambda_max bound

# Signed solid angle per triangle (Van Oosterom-Strackee, apex at origin).
def signed_areas(P, F):
    a = P[F[:, 0]]; b = P[F[:, 1]]; cc = P[F[:, 2]]
    la = np.linalg.norm(a, axis=1); lb = np.linalg.norm(b, axis=1)
    lc = np.linalg.norm(cc, axis=1)
    num = np.sum(a * np.cross(b, cc), axis=1)
    den = (la * lb * lc + np.sum(a * b, axis=1) * lc
           + np.sum(b * cc, axis=1) * la + np.sum(cc * a, axis=1) * lb)
    return 2.0 * np.arctan2(num, den)

def embed_from(phi):
    emb = phi[:, 1:4]
    return emb / np.maximum(np.linalg.norm(emb, axis=1, keepdims=True), 1e-12)

k = 8
n_iter = 40
X0 = rng.standard_normal((n, k))

def subspace_iterate(op, X, iters):
    """Block subspace iteration + Rayleigh-Ritz, ordered by Lt eigenvalue."""
    for _ in range(iters):
        X = op(X)
        X, _ = np.linalg.qr(X)
    Sm = X.T @ Lt @ X
    Sm = 0.5 * (Sm + Sm.T)
    _, wc = np.linalg.eigh(Sm)
    return X @ wc

# Plain reflection: iterate on A = I - Lt/c. Smallest Lt eigenvalues map to
# A-eigenvalues clustered just below 1 -> the fixed-budget power method cannot
# separate phi_0..phi_3 -> the embedding folds.
U_plain = subspace_iterate(lambda X: X - (Lt @ X) / c, X0.copy(), n_iter)
emb_plain = embed_from(inv_sqrt[:, None] * U_plain[:, :4])

# Shift-invert: iterate on (Lt - sigma I)^-1, sigma < 0. Inversion separates
# the clustered small eigenvalues -> the same budget resolves them cleanly.
sigma = -0.01
A_shift = np.linalg.inv(Lt - sigma * np.eye(n))   # dense stand-in for inner CG
U_si = subspace_iterate(lambda X: A_shift @ X, X0.copy(), n_iter)
emb_si = embed_from(inv_sqrt[:, None] * U_si[:, :4])

sa_plain = signed_areas(emb_plain, F)
sa_si = signed_areas(emb_si, F)
for sa in (sa_plain, sa_si):           # orient so "folded" is the minority sign
    if sa.sum() < 0:
        sa *= -1.0
frac_plain = (sa_plain < 0).mean()
frac_si = (sa_si < 0).mean()

fig = plt.figure(figsize=(11, 3.6))

def panel(ax, P, sa, title):
    colors = np.where((sa < 0)[:, None], np.array([0.85, 0.15, 0.15, 1.0]),
                      np.array([0.6, 0.7, 0.85, 1.0]))
    ax.add_collection3d(Poly3DCollection(P[F], facecolors=colors,
                                         edgecolors="k", linewidths=0.1))
    ax.set_xlim(-1, 1); ax.set_ylim(-1, 1); ax.set_zlim(-1, 1)
    ax.set_box_aspect((1, 1, 1)); ax.set_axis_off()
    ax.set_title(title, fontsize=9); ax.view_init(elev=18, azim=30)

panel(fig.add_subplot(1, 3, 1, projection="3d"), emb_plain, sa_plain,
      f"plain reflection\nfolded triangles: {frac_plain*100:.0f}%")
panel(fig.add_subplot(1, 3, 2, projection="3d"), emb_si, sa_si,
      f"shift-invert init\nfolded triangles: {frac_si*100:.0f}%")

ax3 = fig.add_subplot(1, 3, 3)
bins = np.linspace(min(sa_plain.min(), sa_si.min()),
                   max(sa_plain.max(), sa_si.max()), 40)
ax3.hist(sa_plain, bins=bins, alpha=0.6, color="tab:red", label="plain")
ax3.hist(sa_si, bins=bins, alpha=0.6, color="tab:blue", label="shift-invert")
ax3.axvline(0.0, color="k", lw=0.8, ls="--")
ax3.set_xlabel("signed solid angle / triangle (sr)")
ax3.set_ylabel("count"); ax3.legend(fontsize=8)
ax3.set_title("negative = folded", fontsize=9)

fig.suptitle("Spectral spherical embedding: folds and the shift-invert cure",
             fontsize=11)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

### Where this goes next

The spectral embedding is the **intrinsic-geometry** half of the story and the
natural stopping point for this page. What it produces is a fast, recon-surf-grade
spherical map — good enough to seed registration, not yet a strictly fold-free,
anatomically aligned parameterisation. Two further routines live in `sphere.py` and
belong to the **registration unit**; we name and forward-point them, but do not
document them here:

- `spherical_parameterize` — the `mris_sphere` analogue. It takes the spectral
  embedding as its `init='spectral'` and refines it by **Riemannian gradient descent
  on $(S^2)^n$** under a conformal + areal energy, with a **fold-safe backtracking
  line-search** that drives bijectivity to strict and holds it. (Calling it with
  `n_iterations=0` returns the spectral embedding alone.)
- `surface_resample` / `spherical_conv` — moving fields *between* registered
  spheres and convolving on the sphere. These are extrinsic, correspondence-level
  operations, not intrinsic spectral geometry.

The eigenmachinery you have met here — `eigsolve_top_k`, `SolverSpec`, the
shift-invert path — reappears in the [solvers page](solvers.qmd#sec-eigensolve) (the
full solver suite and the implicit VJP) and the
[graph-geometry page](graph-geometry.qmd#sec-spectral-graph) (the same eigensolve on
a graph Laplacian, where you *do* backpropagate through it). Belkin and Niyogi's
**Laplacian eigenmaps** — embedding any data graph by its low Laplacian
eigenfunctions — is the discrete-data sibling of @eq-embedding; the surface case is
the special one where the graph is a triangulated manifold.

## Further reading

- Lévy, B. (2006). *Laplace–Beltrami eigenfunctions: towards an algorithm that
  "understands" geometry.* IEEE International Conference on Shape Modeling and
  Applications (SMI). — the manifesto for manifold harmonics as a geometry-aware
  Fourier basis.
- Reuter, M., Wolter, F.-E., & Peinecke, N. (2006). *Laplace–Beltrami spectra as
  "Shape-DNA" of surfaces and solids.* Computer-Aided Design, 38(4), 342–366. — the
  spectrum as an isometry-invariant shape fingerprint.
- Vallet, B., & Lévy, B. (2008). *Spectral geometry processing with manifold
  harmonics.* Computer Graphics Forum (Eurographics), 27(2), 251–260. — filtering and
  processing fields directly in the harmonic basis.
- Belkin, M., & Niyogi, P. (2003). *Laplacian eigenmaps for dimensionality reduction
  and data representation.* Neural Computation, 15(6), 1373–1396. — the discrete-data
  sibling of spectral embedding.
- Knyazev, A. V. (2001). *Toward the optimal preconditioned eigensolver: locally
  optimal block preconditioned conjugate gradient method (LOBPCG).* SIAM Journal on
  Scientific Computing, 23(2), 517–541. — the iterative eigensolver `eigsolve_top_k`
  is built on.
- Meyer, M., Desbrun, M., Schröder, P., & Barr, A. H. (2003). *Discrete
  differential-geometry operators for triangulated 2-manifolds.* Visualization and
  Mathematics III. — the cotangent operator and mixed-Voronoi mass these eigenpairs
  are computed from.

## See also

- Source: [`geometry/sphere.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/geometry/sphere.py)
  — `spectral_sphere_embedding`, `signed_spherical_areas`, `is_bijective_sphere_map`
  (and the registration-unit `spherical_parameterize`, `surface_resample`,
  `spherical_conv`).
- Source: [`linalg/_eigsolve.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/linalg/_eigsolve.py)
  — `eigsolve_top_k`, `SolverSpec`, `EigPair`, the shift-invert / LOBPCG forwards and
  the shared implicit VJP.
- Source: [`sparse/mesh.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/sparse/mesh.py)
  — `mesh_cotangent_laplacian`, `vertex_areas`, `face_areas` (the $L$ and $M$ this
  page eigendecomposes).
- Previous page: [the Laplace–Beltrami operator](laplace-beltrami.qmd#sec-cotangent)
  — where $L$ and $M$ are built, and [geodesic smoothing](laplace-beltrami.qmd#sec-smoothing).
- Reference tier: [the solvers page](solvers.qmd#sec-eigensolve) — the full eigensolver
  suite; [graph geometry](graph-geometry.qmd#sec-spectral-graph) — the same eigensolve
  on a graph Laplacian.
- Cross-unit: [HSGP](../hsgp.qmd#sec-hsgp) — the bounded-interval twin of the manifold
  harmonics, Laplacian eigenfunctions reweighted by a spectral density.
